source.title: Fatal Well Blowout at Daniel H. Wendland 1-H Well

actions_taken: Emergency response by Caldwell Volunteer Fire Department and Texas Department of Public Safety
Evacuation of personnel from well area
Well shut-in operations by Halliburton Boots & Coots on January 31, 2020
Well plugged and abandoned on April 5, 2021
event.summary: On January 29, 2020, at approximately 3:00 p.m., the Daniel H. Wendland 1-H well in Burleson County, Texas experienced a loss of well control during a workover operation to replace the tubing head. The well blowout released oil and gas which ignited within seconds, resulting in a flash fire that fatally injured three contract workers and seriously injured a fourth. The incident resulted in an estimated $1 million in property damage. Root causes included inadequate well planning, ineffective well control barriers for underpressured reservoirs, and poor ignition source management.
recommendations: Chesapeake Operating, L.L.C. should develop or revise policies incorporating API RP 59 recommendations regarding well planning, specifically including well history review in conjunction with workover well control planning

American Petroleum Institute should publish focused guidance on workover operations including completed wells in underpressured reservoirs, clarify that stabbing valves should not be considered as well control barriers, and emphasize that every well should have two well control barriers with at least one preventative barrier
OSHA should remove the exemption for oil and gas drilling and well servicing from the Control of Hazardous Energy standard (29 CFR 1910.147)

OSHA should promulgate a new standard with prescriptive requirements for primary and secondary barriers, detailed written procedures, management of change requirements, risk assessments, RAGAGEP compliance, Well Construction Interface Documents, flow checks, employee participation, critical well information maintenance, and hazard analysis for onshore oil and gas drilling, production, and servicing/workover activities

threats.name: Influx of Formation Fluids into Wellbore

controls.name: Well Planning and History Review
controls.barrier_role: Identify hazards and plan appropriate well control measures
controls.lod_basis: Industry guidance (API RP 59) recommends well planning prior to workover operations

supporting_text: Chesapeake's well control policies did not incorporate this guidance, and Chesapeake did not adequately review the past history of the Wendland 1-H well, which would have indicated previous well control issues.

A review of the well information, conducted in accordance with industry guidance, could have resulted in the preparation of well control procedures to properly isolate the hydrocarbons from the surface activity.

controls.name: Hydrostatic Pressure Barrier (Brine Injection)
controls.barrier_role: Establish overbalance to prevent formation fluid influx
controls.lod_basis: Standard well control practice for workover operations

supporting_text: The Onsite Supervisor instructed A&L, the hot oil contractor, to pump an additional 50 bbls of 10-pound-per-gallon (ppg) brine into the casing before removing the existing tubing head to establish a hydrostatic barrier.

The 50 bbls of brine temporarily added 1,270 feet, resulting in an overbalance against the formation face of 660 psi, providing sufficient hydrostatic pressure to prevent formation fluids from entering the wellbore.
The intended well control barriers were ineffective in preventing the loss of well control and subsequent blowout.
Industry guidance does not provide reliable well control methods for completed wells in underpressured reservoirs.

controls.name: Stabbing Valve (Open Surface Valve)
controls.barrier_role: Mechanical barrier to prevent uncontrolled flow

supporting_text: The new casing valves and the stabbing valve were left open throughout the installation and bolt-up. These valves were intentionally left open due to concerns about experiencing well pressure while the tubing head was being installed.

At about 3:00 p.m. on Wednesday, January 29, 2020, the installation crew from Eagle was in the process of torquing the bolts attaching the tubing head to the top casing flange when the well began releasing oil and gas through the open stabbing valve and casing valves.
Stabbing valves should not be considered as well control barriers for workover operations

controls.name: Casing Valves (Open Surface Valves)
controls.barrier_role: Mechanical barrier to isolate casing annulus

supporting_text: The new casing valves and the stabbing valve were left open throughout the installation and bolt-up.

At about 3:00 p.m. on Wednesday, January 29, 2020, the installation crew from Eagle was in the process of torquing the bolts attaching the tubing head to the top casing flange when the well began releasing oil and gas through the open stabbing valve and casing valves.
One of the casing valves was plugged at the time of the incident. Although the casing valve was open, the plug prevented the flow of hydrocarbons through the valve.

controls.name: Ignition Source Assessment and Management
controls.lod_basis: Industry guidance recommends hazard assessments for ignition sources near flammable atmospheres

supporting_text: Chesapeake's policies did not incorporate industry guidance recommending that companies should perform hazard assessments when locating ignition sources and atmospheric monitors near potentially flammable atmospheres.
Multiple potential ignition sources were identified in the vicinity of the open wellbore.
At the time of the incident, a mixture of flammable hydrocarbons released from the well and found an ignition source.

controls.name: Atmospheric Monitoring
controls.lod_basis: Industry guidance recommends atmospheric monitors near potentially flammable atmospheres

supporting_text: Chesapeake's policies did not incorporate industry guidance recommending that companies should perform hazard assessments when locating ignition sources and atmospheric monitors near potentially flammable atmospheres.

controls.name: Regulatory Framework for Well Control
controls.barrier_role: Establish mandatory requirements for well control implementation
controls.lod_basis: Federal regulatory requirements for process safety

supporting_text: OSHA has historically exempted onshore oil and gas well drilling and servicing from its Control of Hazardous Energy regulation at 29 CFR 1910.147 as well as its Process Safety Management (PSM) standard requirements under 29 CFR 1910.119.
Existing regulations do not require the implementation and maintenance of well control for onshore oil and gas operations.

The absence of regulations governing onshore oil and gas operations contributed to the incident, resulting in the failure to both effectively control hazardous energy and implement essential risk assessments that could have prevented this incident.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
